label: delphi-r1-p3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from enforcement realities: who would sue, what remedies exist, what actually happens in practice.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not, a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid under article XIII D, section 6(b) — most plausibly by severing/striking the \"conservation-incentive\" increment above the cost-justified (1.6x) portion of the rate, and/or invalidating the revenue-use structure insofar as rebate dollars flow predominantly to non-payers — while likely upholding the debt-service-funded (30%) component as legitimate cost-of-service. Full, root-and-branch invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a partial/substantial strike-down, but the proposition's \"in whole or in substantial part\" framing is satisfied either way given the district's own admissions in the rate study.","rationales":["The rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure derives 'in part' from 'conservation-incentive pricing' rather than strict cost allocation mirrors almost exactly the defect Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 condemned — tier pricing untethered from actual cost-of-service causation — making that increment hard to defend as a permissible fee component.","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 forecloses the district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument since metered water-usage charges are squarely property-related fees under Article XIII D, so the surcharge cannot escape 6(b) scrutiny and must instead survive it on the merits.","The district's concession that most of the 55% rebate allocation benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers gives plaintiffs an independent, easily provable theory under 6(b)(2)'s prohibition on using property-related-fee revenue for purposes unrelated to the parcels charged, separate from any defect in the rate itself.","The documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential is a legitimate, non-trivial cost anchor that gives a court a principled basis to uphold the cost-justified portion of the surcharge while severing only the 'incentive' increment, making full invalidation of the entire charge less likely than a partial/substantial one.","Post-Capistrano authority such as Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2019) 9 Cal.5th 1105 (verify exact holding) reinforces that courts demand individualized cost-causation analysis rather than policy-driven rate design, suggesting a reviewing court will apply searching, plaintiff-favorable scrutiny to this record."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)","Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2019) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify exact holding and applicability to tiered water rates","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1 (Prop 26 tax/fee exceptions) — relevant to alternative tax-characterization argument"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the actual $0.85 rate, quantitatively compared to the 1.6x marginal-cost differential, represents a material, provable excess above cost or is merely rhetorically labeled \"incentive pricing\" while still falling within a defensible cost-of-service range — the hypothetical doesn't supply enough numeric detail (base-tier rate, dollar cost study output) to know how a court would resolve this central factual/evidentiary question on the actual record."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not, a reviewing court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid under article XIII D, section 6(b) — most plausibly by severing/striking the "conservation-incentive" increment above the cost-justified (1.6x) portion of the rate, and/or invalidating the revenue-use structure insofar as rebate dollars flow predominantly to non-payers — while likely upholding the debt-service-funded (30%) component as legitimate cost-of-service. Full, root-and-branch invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a partial/substantial strike-down, but the proposition's "in whole or in substantial part" framing is satisfied either way given the district's own admissions in the rate study.
rationales: The rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure derives 'in part' from 'conservation-incentive pricing' rather than strict cost allocation mirrors almost exactly the defect Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 condemned — tier pricing untethered from actual cost-of-service causation — making that increment hard to defend as a permissible fee component.

Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 forecloses the district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument since metered water-usage charges are squarely property-related fees under Article XIII D, so the surcharge cannot escape 6(b) scrutiny and must instead survive it on the merits.

The district's concession that most of the 55% rebate allocation benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers gives plaintiffs an independent, easily provable theory under 6(b)(2)'s prohibition on using property-related-fee revenue for purposes unrelated to the parcels charged, separate from any defect in the rate itself.

The documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential is a legitimate, non-trivial cost anchor that gives a court a principled basis to uphold the cost-justified portion of the surcharge while severing only the 'incentive' increment, making full invalidation of the entire charge less likely than a partial/substantial one.

Post-Capistrano authority such as Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2019) 9 Cal.5th 1105 (verify exact holding) reinforces that courts demand individualized cost-causation analysis rather than policy-driven rate design, suggesting a reviewing court will apply searching, plaintiff-favorable scrutiny to this record.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2019) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify exact holding and applicability to tiered water rates
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1 (Prop 26 tax/fee exceptions) — relevant to alternative tax-characterization argument
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the actual $0.85 rate, quantitatively compared to the 1.6x marginal-cost differential, represents a material, provable excess above cost or is merely rhetorically labeled "incentive pricing" while still falling within a defensible cost-of-service range — the hypothetical doesn't supply enough numeric detail (base-tier rate, dollar cost study output) to know how a court would resolve this central factual/evidentiary question on the actual record.
